Build prompt · Agents architecture

Modular Movemental agents — author-swappable retrieval, prompts, and tools

This document is both a product/engineering brief and a paste-ready implementer prompt. It describes how ai-lab-agent, movemental-ai-agents, the Alan Hirsch tenant app (AI Lab UI), and this movemental-ai repo relate — and the remaining work to make every agent configurable per author/tenant without code forks.

Goals (definition of done)

  • Author swap: Replace corpus, voice layer, file-search / vector backend, and model defaults by changing data + config, not scattered code edits.
  • Tenant isolation: Every runtime binding resolves through organization_id (or equivalent tenant key) from DB or signed server config.
  • Single orchestration contract: All agents (AI Lab, companions, coaches, content agents) resolve prompts, tools, and retrieval from the same abstraction layer.
  • Operational clarity: Dashboard or migrations can clone an “agent profile” from one tenant to another with explicit overrides.

1. Repository map (same ecosystem, different responsibilities)

The following repos are referenced across Movemental docs; they are not all inside the movemental-ai workspace clone.

Surface Role today Modularity gap
movemental-ai (this repo) Org/marketing site; Drizzle schema includes agents, agent_tools, agent_tool_assignments, guardrails, traces; simplified REST under /api/simplified/*; AI Lab conversation tables (ai_lab_*). Schema supports configurability, but runtime agent execution for the tenant AI Lab does not live here. Risk of two sources of truth unless services converge.
movemental-ai-agents Central agent runtime (npm ai-agents): tool wiring, OpenAI Agents SDK patterns, service API auth, Redis, DB access — documented as requiring TENANT_ORG_ID, Supabase keys, etc. Implementation detail for retrieval (OpenAI file_search vs Supabase/pgvector vs Gemini) likely hard-coded or env-driven per deployment; must become tenant-scoped rows + adapters.
ai-lab-agent Focused service for AI Lab traffic (OpenAI-centric paths per bootstrap docs). Almost certainly author-specific prompts and vector store IDs unless fully DB-driven.
alan-hirsch tenant app Public UX: /ai-lab, notebook, corpus helpers (e.g. Gemini upload scripts referenced in internal docs). UI + client wiring may assume Alan corpus and voice; tenant config must drive labels and agent endpoints.
movemental-visual-editor Studio: authoring, agents UI (per Alan Hirsch platform README in this repo’s docs). Needs parity fields for “corpus binding” and prompt packs when editing agents.
Browser (AI Lab UI) │ ▼ ┌───────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────────┐ │ Tenant Next app │────▶│ ai-lab-agent / │ │ (alan-hirsch) │ │ movemental-ai-agents │ └───────────────────┘ └───────────┬────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ Supabase Postgres │ │ agents · tools · │ │ assignments · orgs │ └─────────────────────┘

Canonical bootstrap references: docs/build/plans/movemental-multi-repo-env-bootstrap.md and docs/projects/alan-hirsch/README.md.

2. Database reality check (Movemental Supabase project)

Live inspection (read-only) of the Movemental-hosted database shows useful signals about how far configurability has progressed:

  • agents rows exist with slugs such as ai-lab-agent, voice-coach, formation-companion, seo-expert, etc.
  • Tenant concentration: At time of investigation, all agent definitions observed belonged to a single organizations row (the Alan Hirsch tenant). Other organizations in organizations did not yet have parallel agent rows — so multi-tenant agent catalogs are not populated.
  • agents.metadata: Mostly empty objects for key slugs (e.g. AI Lab). One legacy note existed for voice-coach migration provenance — not a structured retrieval binding.
  • agent_tools: Definitions exist (parameter JSON schemas for tools like search_corpus). Tool rows are scoped to the same organization as above.
  • agent_tool_assignments: Example — ai-lab-agent is assigned search_corpus with an empty per-assignment config object. That means the runtime must be inferring corpus routing from code/env, not from assignment config.
Implication: The relational model is a strong foundation, but author-swappable behavior is not yet encoded in rows. Until bindings live in metadata, tool_config/config, or new normalized tables, swapping authors requires engineering changes across services.

3. Coupling hotspots (what blocks “swap author” today)

3.1 Prompts

  • Long system_prompt text in agents still reads Alan-specific / Movemental-default in previews — acceptable for one tenant, fragile for N tenants.
  • No versioning UX tied to releases; version column exists but operational workflow unclear.
  • Layered prompts (identity · theme · mode · style) should be composable blobs keyed by slug, not one monolithic string — aligns with internal skills (write-instructions, agent-instructions).

3.2 Retrieval & file search

  • OpenAI vector store / file_search IDs are naturally deployment-specific; they must map to CorpusBinding records per org (see §4), not env globals.
  • Alan tenant docs describe alternate stacks (Gemini File API, Vertex RAG). Multiple backends imply an adapter interface in movemental-ai-agents, not conditional spaghetti per route.
  • search_corpus tool parameters include book_filter — domain-specific to book corpus; other authors may need tags, collections, or media types. Parameter schemas may need tenant_extension or tool variants.

3.3 Tools & guardrails

  • agent_tools.implementation may be null in DB while code registers handlers by name — fragile rename surface.
  • Guardrails and handoffs tables exist in schema; ensure every agent class uses them consistently (not only DB-backed agents).

3.4 Conversation storage

  • ai_lab_conversations carries theme, mode, style, organization_id — good for UX state; tie these enums to validated catalog rows so new authors cannot drift strings silently.

4. Target modular model (recommended abstractions)

Introduce explicit concepts (names illustrative — implement as tables or structured JSON with Zod validation):

4.1 AuthorProfile / tenant AI persona

  • FK: organization_id
  • Fields: display name, public bio snippet, voice marker IDs, forbidden topics, citation format
  • Links to default model routing profile

4.2 CorpusBinding

  • FK: organization_id
  • provider: openai_vector_store | openai_file_search | supabase_pgvector | gemini_file | vertex_rag | …
  • provider_resource_id (encrypted-at-rest if sensitive)
  • filter_defaults JSON (e.g. book slugs, series IDs)
  • health_check_policy (last sync, embedding model version)

4.3 PromptPack

  • Versioned layers: base, voice, safety, product_surface (ai-lab vs companion)
  • Rendered → final system string server-side; hash stored on traces

4.4 AgentRuntimeSpec

Either extend agents.metadata with a strict schema or add a child table:

{
  "slug": "ai-lab-agent",
  "corpus_binding_id": "uuid",
  "prompt_pack_id": "uuid",
  "model_defaults": { "primary": "gpt-5.x", "fallback": "gpt-4.1" },
  "tool_allowlist_ref": "uuid",
  "feature_flags": { "notebook_enabled": true }
}

4.5 Uniform resolver API (inside movemental-ai-agents)

Pseudocode contract:

resolveAgentRuntime({ organizationId, agentSlug, surface })
  → { systemPrompt, tools[], retrievalAdapter, modelParams }

Every HTTP handler and worker calls this once per request; no parallel shortcut paths.

5. Phased work backlog

Phase A — Schema & validation (no behavior change)

  1. Add Zod schemas for agents.metadata, agent_tools.config, and agent_tool_assignments.config documenting allowed keys.
  2. Migration: create corpus_bindings (or equivalent) + optional prompt_packs / prompt_layers.
  3. Backfill: Alan Hirsch tenant — move vector store / retrieval IDs from env into binding rows; keep env as dev-only override.

Phase B — Runtime resolver (movemental-ai-agents + ai-lab-agent)

  1. Implement resolveAgentRuntime with caching (Redis key: org + slug + prompt pack version).
  2. Teach search_corpus (and siblings) to read CorpusBinding + assignment config overrides.
  3. Align ai-lab-agent with the same resolver library (shared package or git submodule) to eliminate drift.

Phase C — Multi-tenant catalog

  1. For each org that needs AI: clone “template agent set” with org FKs; parameterize slugs or use namespaced slugs (ai-lab-agent__orgSlug) — pick one convention and enforce uniqueness.
  2. Studio UI: forms for corpus binding, prompt layers, tool toggles.

Phase D — Tenant apps & org site

  1. Alan Hirsch app: read agent slug + API base URL from tenant.config.ts (or DB-backed tenant settings) — zero hardcoded “Alan” in shared components.
  2. movemental-ai: if it exposes agents API publicly, gate by org membership and document that it mirrors the same resolver semantics.

Phase E — Observability & safety

  1. Emit trace metadata: corpus binding ID, prompt hash, embedding model.
  2. Guardrails from DB mandatory on external-facing routes.

6. Acceptance criteria (binary checks)

  • New organization can receive a working ai-lab-agent by inserting rows only + valid corpus credentials — no new TypeScript for standard cases.
  • Switching Alan’s vector store ID requires a DB update or studio save, not redeploy (except when secrets rotate).
  • Renaming a tool in code fails CI unless DB implementation map or registry is updated (contract test).
  • All agents that call retrieval use the same adapter interface with unit tests per provider.

7. Risks & non-goals

  • Risk — dual runtime: If ai-lab-agent and movemental-ai-agents diverge, modularity regresses. Prefer one shared npm workspace package for resolution logic.
  • Risk — secret sprawl: Corpus provider keys must stay server-side; bindings reference IDs, not raw files in JSON.
  • Non-goal: Forcing every author onto OpenAI file_search; the abstraction must allow Gemini-first tenants.

8. Paste-ready implementer prompt (for coding agents / engineers)

Use this block verbatim to drive implementation work in movemental-ai-agents, ai-lab-agent, tenant apps, and migrations.

You are implementing multi-tenant, author-swappable AI agents for Movemental.

Context:
- Postgres schema already includes agents, agent_tools, agent_tool_assignments, agent_guardrails, agent_handoffs, and ai_lab_* conversation tables on the shared Movemental Supabase project.
- Live data currently concentrates agent rows under one organization; metadata and per-assignment tool config are sparse — retrieval routing still depends on code/env.
- Repos: movemental-ai-agents (primary runtime), ai-lab-agent (AI Lab entrypoint), alan-hirsch (AI Lab UI), movemental-ai (schema + simplified APIs).

Objective:
Make prompts, corpus/file-search backends, and tool wiring configurable per organization_id without code forks.

Requirements:
1. Introduce a typed CorpusBinding (or equivalent) with provider enum and resource IDs; migrate Alan tenant values from environment variables into DB rows with dev overrides.
2. Implement resolveAgentRuntime({ organizationId, agentSlug }) in a shared module used by both movemental-ai-agents and ai-lab-agent. It must assemble system prompt from versioned PromptPack layers and attach tools from DB assignments + configs.
3. Extend agent_tool_assignments.config to carry per-agent overrides (limits, default filters). Validate with Zod at load time.
4. Add CI contract tests: every tool name in DB maps to a registered handler; every agent slug used by the tenant app exists for that org.
5. Document clone workflow for provisioning a new organization’s agent set from templates.

Deliver migrations, runtime code, and minimal studio/dashboard fields to edit corpus bindings and prompt pack versions. Avoid duplicating author-specific strings in React components — tenant config only.

Non-goals: Changing UX copy for movemental marketing site unless needed for API wiring.

Investigation notes: Movemental Supabase agents/tools snapshot and multi-repo map — internal engineering prompt. Update database observations whenever tenant rollout expands beyond a single-org agent catalog.
